privacy.title: Privacy, Yet Another Quiz
privacy.heading: Privacy
privacy.intro: We store a random device ID your browser makes the first time it needs one, plus the game data linked to that ID, daily submissions, quiz personal bests, tic-tac-toe outcomes, an optional nickname. Retention runs from 48 hours (leaderboard rows) to 1 year (daily submissions); profile + records stay until you ask us to delete them. We also use Cloudflare Web Analytics, anonymous, cookieless traffic counts only (page views, country, browser), to see how many people visit. No ads, no accounts, no cookies.

removal.title: Want your data gone? You have three options.
removal.bomb: Drop a bomb on the Azure Cosmos DB datacenter in Amsterdam.
removal.invade: Invade and seize every Cosmos DB geo-replica on the planet.
removal.request: Your profile gets flagged for removal and we wipe it manually within 30 days. If you come back and play in the meantime, we'll assume you changed your mind and cancel it.
removal.confirmed: Got it, your data is flagged for removal. Coming back to play will cancel the request.
removal.error: Could not send the request. Please try again later.

ttt.title: Yet Another Quiz: Tic Tac Toe with Country Flags
ttt.offlineTitle: Yet Another Quiz: Offline Tic Tac Toe
ttt.waitingShareCode: Waiting for opponent… share the code above
ttt.rulesBody: Each cell sits on a row and a column. Pick a country whose flag matches both, for example, an "Asia" row and a "red" column needs a flag that is both Asian and red. First to line up three wins.

ttt9x9.title: Yet Another Quiz: 9×9 Flag Tic Tac Toe
ttt9x9.offlineTitle: Yet Another Quiz: Offline 9×9 Flag Tic Tac Toe
ttt9x9.rulesBody: Nine small tic-tac-toe boards forming a big 3×3 board. Each cell sits on a row and a column, pick a country whose flag matches both. Win a small board to claim it; line up three claimed boards on the big board to win.

flagsdata.title: Yet Another Quiz: All Country Flags

flagFacts.imageCredit: Flag images: flag-icons and Wikimedia Commons

al.intro: Albania's flag is a black double-headed eagle on a deep red field. The red is for bravery and the blood shed for freedom; the eagle stands for the Albanians themselves. The bird is ancient. It was the emblem of the Byzantine Empire, taken up in the 15th century by the national hero Skanderbeg as the arms of his House of Kastrioti while he held off the Ottoman Empire. The eagle has stayed at the centre of the flag ever since. Through a turbulent century, as principality gave way to kingdom, to occupation, and to communism, what kept changing was what appeared around it.
al.principality: The new Principality of Albania set a white star above the eagle.
al.restored: After the foreign prince left, the re-established state flew the bare eagle again.
al.republic: The first Albanian Republic (1926–1928) flew a compact black double-headed eagle.
al.kingdom: King Zog's monarchy crowned the eagle with the golden helmet of Skanderbeg.
al.italian: Fascist Italy's occupation framed the eagle with two lictor's fasces.
al.crowned: Its royal state flag set an ornate crown above the eagle.
al.wartime: The communist-led wartime government added a gold hammer and sickle in the canton.
al.communist: Communist Albania added a gold-bordered red star above the eagle.
al.current: With the fall of communism the star came off, leaving the eagle alone again.

fact.feathers: The eagle carries exactly 25 feathers, nine on each wing and seven in the tail. Tradition holds that the number honours Skanderbeg, who fought the Ottomans for 25 years and in 25 battles.
fact.landofeagles: Albanians call their country Shqipëria, popularly read as the "Land of the Eagles", and themselves shqiptarë, the "sons of the eagle". Few nations are so closely identified with the bird on their flag.

gb.intro:
The Union Jack is really three flags stacked into one. It layers the red cross of St George (England) over the white diagonal saltire of St Andrew (Scotland), with the red saltire of St Patrick (Ireland) threaded through, the badges of three patron saints, combined as their kingdoms were joined by crown and parliament.

One nation is missing: Wales. By the time the flag was first assembled in 1606, Wales had already been annexed to the Kingdom of England, so it was never given a place of its own.
gb.union1606: England's St George's cross combined with Scotland's St Andrew's saltire, made when James VI of Scotland also became James I of England.
gb.current: St Patrick's red saltire (for Ireland) added to the 1606 flag in 1801, giving the asymmetric Union Jack still flown today.

fact.wales: Wales isn't represented at all. It had been absorbed into the Kingdom of England long before the union, so its red dragon never made it onto the flag.
fact.name: Why "Jack"? A jack is a small flag flown from the bow of a ship, the design spent much of its early life at sea, and the nickname stuck.
fact.asymmetry: Look closely and it isn't symmetric: the broad white diagonal sits above the red on the hoist side and below it on the fly. That "counterchange" is why there's a right way up.
fact.distress: Because it has a right way up, flying it upside-down is a recognised distress signal, and, done by accident, a minor national embarrassment.
fact.offspring: It's the most-copied flag in the world: Australia, New Zealand, Fiji, Tuvalu and the US state of Hawaii all still carry a Union Jack in the corner.

gb.walesProposal: One proposed version added the Welsh dragon, but it was never adopted.
gb.walesProposalAlt: A Union Jack with a red Welsh dragon in the centre.

gb-eng.intro:
England's flag is the cross of St George: a plain red cross on white. St George, a Roman soldier martyred in the eastern Mediterranean around 303 AD, who never set foot in England, became the country's patron in the Middle Ages, and his emblem was carried by English soldiers from the age of the Crusades.

The same red cross sits at the centre of the Union Jack, England's contribution to the flag of the United Kingdom.
gb-eng.lions: Before the cross, England's royal banner was three golden lions on red, the arms of the medieval kings, still worn today by England's football and cricket teams.
gb-eng.current: The cross of St George, adopted as England's emblem during the 13th century and essentially unchanged since.

fact.george: St George wasn't English: he was a Greek-speaking Roman officer from Cappadocia (modern Turkey), and is shared as a patron by Georgia, Catalonia, Ethiopia and more.
fact.genoa: England is said to have adopted the red cross from Genoa, flying the Italian city's flag so its ships could claim the protection of Genoa's powerful fleet.
fact.union: It forms the centre of the Union Jack, the red upright cross laid over the Scottish and Irish saltires.
fact.lions: The three lions trace to Richard the Lionheart's royal seal around 1198; it is one of the oldest coats of arms still in continuous use.

gb-sct.intro:
Scotland's flag, the Saltire, is the white diagonal cross of St Andrew on a blue field, by tradition one of the oldest national flags in the world. Legend sets its origin at the Battle of Athelstaneford in 832 AD, when a Pictish-Scots king is said to have seen a white saltire of cloud in the blue sky before victory, and took it as his banner.

St Andrew, the apostle martyred on an X-shaped cross, is Scotland's patron; his diagonal cross joined England's upright one to form the first Union Flag in 1606.
gb-sct.lion: Scotland's other great flag: the Royal Standard, a red lion rampant inside a double border. It is the banner of the monarch, its use is technically restricted by law.
gb-sct.current: The Saltire, St Andrew's white cross on blue, the national flag of Scotland.

fact.oldest: By the 832 legend the Saltire is often called the oldest national flag still in use; its blue and white have been recorded for over 800 years.
fact.andrew: St Andrew was crucified on an X-shaped cross at his own request, feeling unworthy to die as Jesus had, that diagonal became his emblem across the Christian world.
fact.union: St Andrew's saltire is the white diagonal in the Union Jack, beneath England's red cross.
fact.lion: The red Lion Rampant is the royal banner: strictly it belongs to the monarch, though Scots fly it enthusiastically at sport regardless.

gb-wls.intro:
Wales flies Y Ddraig Goch, "the Red Dragon", a red dragon on a field of white over green. The dragon is one of Britain's oldest symbols, tied to the Welsh kings and, in the tale of Merlin, to a red dragon that fought a white one beneath the earth and won, an omen of the Welsh prevailing over the invading Saxons.

Uniquely among the home nations, Wales is absent from the Union Jack, so its dragon stands entirely on its own.
gb-wls.david: An older Welsh flag is the cross of St David, a gold cross on black, for the country's patron saint, still flown on St David's Day.
gb-wls.current: Y Ddraig Goch, the Red Dragon, became the official flag of Wales only in 1959, though the symbol is many centuries older.

fact.union: Wales is the only home nation missing from the Union Jack: it had been annexed to England before the union flags were designed, so it was folded in under England's cross.
fact.dragon: The red dragon may descend from the Roman cavalry standard (the draco); legend has it carried by kings like Cadwaladr and Owain Glyndŵr, and the Tudor dynasty flew it too.
fact.official: Despite its age, the dragon became the official national flag only in 1959, a very young official flag for a very old symbol.
fact.dragons: Wales's red dragon and Bhutan's white thunder-dragon are the only two dragons flying on a country's flag today.

ie.intro:
Ireland's tricolour of green, white and orange is a flag about reconciliation. Green stands for the Gaelic and Catholic tradition, orange for the Protestant tradition of William of Orange, and the white between them for a lasting peace between the two.

It was first flown in 1848 by the nationalist Thomas Francis Meagher, modelled on the French Tricolore he had seen in revolutionary Paris.
ie.royal: Under the English then British crown, Ireland's banner was a gold harp on a royal-blue field, the arms of the Kingdom of Ireland from Henry VIII in the 1540s until the 1801 union. The blue is still called Saint Patrick's blue.
ie.harp: The green harp flag, first flown by Owen Roe O'Neill and Confederate Ireland in 1642. The same gold harp, moved from royal blue to green, became the banner of Irish nationalism for the next two centuries.
ie.patrick: The red saltire of St Patrick, Ireland's element of the Union Jack. Its history is disputed: some argue it was devised simply to give Ireland a place on the British flag.
ie.erin: "Erin go Bragh" (Ireland Forever): the green harp flag with its motto added. It was carried by the Saint Patrick's Battalion in the Mexican-American War of 1847, and in 1906 the athlete Peter O'Connor climbed a flagpole to fly it at the Olympics.
ie.current: The green-white-orange tricolour, flown from 1848 and made the national flag of the Irish state in 1937.

fact.colours: The three bands are a deliberate handshake: green for the Catholic/nationalist tradition, orange for the Protestant/unionist one, and white for peace between them.
fact.meagher: Thomas Francis Meagher unveiled it in 1848, inspired by the French Tricolore; the silk was reportedly a gift from "the women of France" to the Irish cause.
fact.harp: Ireland is the only country whose emblem is a musical instrument. The harp is on its coins, its passports, and the Guinness logo (which faces the other way).
fact.crowned: Under the monarchy the harp was usually drawn with a crown on top, on coins, seals and police badges. When Ireland became independent it deliberately dropped the crown, and the emblem has stayed uncrowned ever since. The same gold harp on royal blue flies today as the personal flag of the President of Ireland.
fact.otherflags: The tricolour is not Ireland's only flag. The Starry Plough, a plough traced in stars, is the banner of the labour movement; the orange Sunburst belongs to the republican Fianna; and the Four Provinces flag quarters the arms of Ulster, Munster, Leinster and Connacht.
fact.order: The order matters: green always goes next to the pole. Flown the other way round it counts as a different, incorrect flag, and, to some, a pointed insult.

ch.intro:
Few flags are as old, or as square, as Switzerland's. The white cross on a red field was a battlefield mark of the Old Swiss Confederacy centuries before it belonged to a country: it is first recorded at the Battle of Laupen in 1339, where confederate soldiers sewed white crosses onto their clothes to tell friend from foe.

The design goes back further still, to the war banner of the Holy Roman Empire, which bore the same white cross on red, the white long read as the purity of the cause, the red as the blood of the martyrs. What began as a war sign became, five centuries later, the flag of modern Switzerland.
ch.imperial: The white cross on red as the Old Swiss Confederacy's heraldic emblem, by legend drawn from the war banner of the Holy Roman Empire, and worn into battles like Laupen (1339).
ch.flamme: Confederate cantons carried square battle standards in the flammé style, flame-like tongues radiating from a central cross, each canton in its own colours. This one is Bern's red and black.
ch.helvetic: When France invaded in 1798, the puppet Helvetic Republic swapped the cross for a green-red-yellow tricolour. Imposed from outside, it lasted only about five years.
ch.current: After Napoleon's fall the cantons returned to the white cross on red. General Dufour unified it for the federal army, and the 1848 constitution made it the flag of the new federal state, square, as it remains.

fact.square: Switzerland is one of only two countries with a square national flag. The other is Vatican City. The one exception is the flag flown on Swiss lakes and rivers, which is an ordinary rectangle.
fact.redcross: The Red Cross emblem is simply the Swiss flag reversed, a red cross on white, adopted in 1864 to honour Switzerland, home of the movement's founder Henry Dunant.
fact.proportions: The white cross doesn't touch the edges: its four arms are equal, and each arm is one-sixth longer than it is wide.
fact.red2017: The exact shade of red went undefined by law until 2017, when Switzerland finally fixed it, a pure red, close to Pantone 485.
fact.dufour: General Guillaume-Henri Dufour, who standardised the flag, was also a cartographer and engineer; Switzerland's highest peak, the Dufourspitze, is named after him.

gr.intro:
For centuries Greece was ruled by the Ottoman Empire, yet Greek merchant ships still sailed under their own colours. From that maritime tradition, and from the Orthodox cross at the heart of Greek identity, grew the flag Greece flies today. For 150 years two flags flew side by side, a white cross on land and blue-and-white stripes at sea, until the stripes won out in 1978.

Curiously, the exact shade of blue has never been fixed by law, over the centuries it has drifted between pale sky-blue and deep navy.
gr.ottoman: Under Ottoman rule, Greek Orthodox merchant ships flew a red flag crossed by a blue stripe.
gr.revolution: The sky-blue cross on white, the banner most flown in the 1821 War of Independence.
gr.land: The land flag adopted in 1822: a white cross on blue. Governor Kapodistrias fixed its darker blue in 1828; it flew over the Greek state until 1978.
gr.crowned: When Greece became a kingdom, a golden crown was set at the centre of the cross. It came and went with the throne, gone in the republics, back in 1935, and removed for good when the monarchy fell in 1973.
gr.warensign: At sea the striped flag carried the same crown in its canton, the kingdom's naval war ensign for a century.
gr.junta: The colonels' military dictatorship darkened the blue to a deep navy, its version of the flag flown from 1970 to 1975.
gr.current: Nine blue-and-white stripes with a cross in the canton. Long the flag at sea, in 1978 it replaced the cross on land too, becoming Greece's sole national flag.

fact.stripes: The nine stripes carry the revolutionaries' battle cry: the five blue stripes are the syllables of «Ελευθερία» (Freedom), the four white ones «ή Θάνατος» (or Death).
fact.cross: The white cross stands for the Greek Orthodox Church, the institution that kept the Greek language and identity alive through nearly four centuries of Ottoman rule.
fact.colours: Blue is said to be the Greek sea and sky, white the waves and the purity of the struggle, though Greece's own 1934 encyclopedia admitted the true origin of the colours was never proven.
fact.ratio: The flag's proportions are set by law: a 2 : 3 ratio, with each arm of the cross one-fifth of the flag's width.
fact.guinness: The largest Greek flag in the world flies at Cape Keri on the island of Zakynthos: around 670 square metres of blue and white that entered the Guinness Book of Records in 2007.

pl.intro:
Poland's colours come straight from the state coat of arms: the White Eagle sits on the upper white stripe, a symbol of purity and nobility, while the lower red stripe echoes the colour of the heraldic shield, standing for courage and valour.

The white eagle has flown over Poland since the Middle Ages: on the swallow-tailed banner of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth, on the flags the 19th-century uprisings carried against the partitioning powers, and, once the republic was reborn in 1919, distilled down to the plain two colours it flies today.
pl.eagle: The White Eagle on a red shield, the flag's white and red come straight from it.
pl.commonwealth: The Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth flew a red-and-white swallow-tailed banner charged with the joint arms of both nations.
pl.january: The 1863 uprising against Russian rule fought under a white-red-blue flag bearing the arms of Poland, Lithuania and Ruthenia.
pl.current: The plain white-over-red bicolour, the national flag since 1919.

fact.crown: Communist Poland stripped the crown from the eagle's head in 1955; when communism fell in 1990 the crown was put back, where it remains today.
fact.lookalikes: Flip Poland's flag top-to-bottom and you get Indonesia and Monaco, red over white. Those two are near-identical themselves, told apart only by their proportions: Indonesia is the longer 2:3, Monaco the squarer 4:5.

ge.intro: Georgia's five-cross flag looks brand new (it was only adopted in 2004) but its design is medieval. A large St George's cross divides the field, with a smaller "Bolnisi" cross in each corner, together forming the shape known as the Jerusalem Cross. The Bolnisi cross itself traces back to stone carvings on a Georgian church from the 5th century, and a European map from 1367 already shows this banner flying over Tbilisi.
ge.republic: The first Georgian republic's flag, wine-red with a black-and-white canton, designed by the painter Iakob Nikoladze, winner of a national contest.
ge.soviet: After the Red Army invaded in 1921, Soviet symbols took over: a red banner carrying the hammer, sickle and star.
ge.restored: When the Soviet Union collapsed, the 1918 republic flag returned in December 1991.
ge.current: This five-cross banner was the flag of the Rose Revolution itself, the opposition's symbol, waved by the crowds in 2003. Months after that peaceful change of power, it became the national flag in 2004.

fact.crosses: The five crosses are usually read as the Five Wounds of Christ, or as Christ, the central cross, among the four evangelists.
fact.jerusalem: That one-big-plus-four-small shape is the "Jerusalem Cross", an emblem of the Crusades, echoing Georgia's deep medieval Christian ties, when Georgian knights also took up the cross.
fact.stgeorge: The big central cross is St George's Cross, the same red-cross-on-white as England's flag. Fitting, since St George is Georgia's patron saint.

fr.intro:
For centuries France flew the flags of its kings: the flame-red Oriflamme carried into battle, a deep blue field strewn with golden fleurs-de-lis, and at last a plain white royal flag.

The Tricolour was born in the Revolution. Blue and red are the old colours of Paris; white was the colour of the king. In July 1789 the Marquis de Lafayette combined them in a single cockade, city and crown in one badge, and that blue-white-red soon became the flag of the new nation.
fr.oriflamme: Before it had a flag, France had the Oriflamme, the flame-red war banner of the kings, raised at the abbey of Saint-Denis before battle. By tradition no enemy could stand against it while it flew.
fr.ancient: The banner of the Capetian kings, a deep blue field sown with countless golden fleurs-de-lis, the lily long tied to the Virgin Mary and the emblem of the French crown.
fr.modern: In the 14th century the scattered lilies were reduced to three, by tradition for the Holy Trinity. This blue-and-gold arms served the monarchy until the Revolution.
fr.royal: White was the colour of the French monarchy, the royal flag itself was white, here strewn with the golden fleurs-de-lis of the king's arms, a tradition running back to Joan of Arc's white banner.
fr.firsttricolour: The first Tricolour, arranged in 1790, carried red at the hoist, not blue. In this order it flew over the early Revolution, the fall of the monarchy and the young Republic.
fr.current: Paris's blue and red joined to the king's white, fixed in this order in 1794 and carried through the Revolution and Napoleon's Empire.
fr.white: With Napoleon defeated and the monarchy restored, the Bourbons brought back the plain white royal flag, the drapeau blanc, in place of the Tricolour.
fr.restored: The July Revolution of 1830 swept the Bourbons away for good, and Louis-Philippe, the "Citizen King", restored the Tricolour. Its blue was the traditional dark navy.
fr.lighter: In 1976 President Giscard d'Estaing had the flag's blue and red lightened, a brighter blue closer to the European flag that also read better on colour television. This lighter version flew for over forty years.
fr.navy2020: In 2020 France quietly switched back to the darker navy "bleu marine", the shade of the Revolution. The change was so subtle that most people never noticed the flag had changed at all.

fact.meaning: France has never fixed an official meaning to the three colours. The familiar liberté, égalité, fraternité reading came later; the older explanation is simpler: white for the king, blue and red for Paris.
fact.paris: Paris has flown blue and red since at least 1358. Tradition ties them to two saints: blue to St Martin, who cut his cloak in two for a beggar in the snow, and red to St Denis, the city's patron.
fact.flip: When the stripes were first arranged in 1790, red sat at the hoist. The painter Jacques-Louis David flipped the order to today's blue-white-red in 1794.
fact.chambord: In 1873 the monarchists held enough seats to restore the throne, but their candidate, the Comte de Chambord, refused to reign unless France gave up the Tricolour for the white flag of his ancestors. Neither side would yield, so France remained a republic. On hearing it, the Pope reportedly sighed: "And all that, for a napkin."
fact.wwii: In the Second World War two Frances flew two flags. Vichy kept the plain Tricolour, its head of state Marshal Pétain flying a personal standard marked with a francisque, a double-headed axe. General de Gaulle's Free French added the red Cross of Lorraine, which became the badge of the Resistance.
fact.influence: The Tricolour became one of the most copied flags in history, from the 1800s on, newly independent nations across Europe and beyond took up three-stripe designs as a badge of national, often republican, identity.

fr.bourbonArms: During the Restoration the plain white flag was France's national flag, but the Bourbon kings also flew this richer version, the white drapeau blanc bearing their crowned coat of arms. It was this white-flag tradition that Chambord refused to give up in 1873.
fr.bourbonArmsAlt: White flag of the Bourbon Restoration bearing the crowned Bourbon coat of arms.

af.intro:
No country has changed its flag as restlessly as Afghanistan. It was redrawn more than twenty times, almost every coup or change of ruler bringing new colours, a new emblem, or an entirely new banner.

The story runs from the plain black banners of the old dynasties and the green flag of the Durrani empire, through a recurring black-red-green tricolour (black for the troubled past, red for the blood of independence, green for hope and Islam), to the red of the communists, the green-white-black of the mujahideen and the white Shahada flag of the Taliban. What follows walks the whole run.
af.black: Before it was a single state, the lands of Afghanistan flew plain black banners. The Hotak dynasty, rising from Kandahar in the early 18th century, carried a simple black flag.
af.durrani: Ahmad Shah Durrani founded the Afghan empire in 1747. His dynasty flew a green field split by a white stripe, green being the colour of Islam.
af.black1880: Under Abdur Rahman Khan, the "Iron Emir" who fixed Afghanistan's modern borders, the emirate returned to a plain black flag.
af.emirate: Habibullah Khan placed the first emblem on the black flag: a white mosque and mihrab set inside a wreath.
af.octagram: Amanullah Khan, who won full independence from Britain in 1919, framed the emblem inside a white eight-pointed star.
af.emblem1921: The emblem was redrawn and the flag's proportions changed, still white on a black field.
af.emblem1926: The star was dropped and the emblem enlarged, the last of the black flags before colour arrived.
af.horiz1928: Amanullah Khan's very first 1928 flag laid the black, red and green as horizontal bands, before the design was turned on its side later the same year.
af.tricolour: In 1928 King Amanullah Khan, freshly back from a tour of Europe, swapped the black royal banner for a black-red-green vertical tricolour, the first of the tricolours that would keep coming back for the rest of the century.
af.variant1928: A variant of the vertical tricolour flew the same colours with the state emblem drawn differently.
af.radial1929: Through the turmoil of 1929 the tricolour reappeared with a mosque emblem set inside a white starburst.
af.kalakani: A Tajik rebel, Habibullah Kalakani, seized Kabul for nine months in 1929 and flew a striped red-black-white flag, its colours nodding to older banners.
af.nadir: Nadir Shah drove Kalakani out and restored the black-red-green tricolour, now under a new royal emblem.
af.kingdom: The tricolour of the Kingdom of Afghanistan, its mosque-and-wheat emblem in the centre. Some version of this vertical flag flew for over forty years, the country's longest-lived design by far.
af.republic1973: Mohammed Daoud Khan's 1973 coup ended the monarchy. His first republican flag kept the vertical tricolour, removing only the date beneath the mosque.
af.daoud: A year later Daoud's Republic redrew the flag as horizontal black-red-green, charged with the eagle of the revolution.
af.daoudvert: Daoud's republic also used a vertical version of its eagle flag.
af.noemblem: The April 1978 communist coup first flew the bare tricolour, with no emblem at all, for a few months.
af.red: The 1978 communist coup brought an almost entirely red flag in the Soviet mould, the gold letters in the canton spelling Khalq, "the masses". It lasted barely two years.
af.dra: The pro-Soviet government dropped the red flag and restored black-red-green, now in horizontal bands and stamped with a new socialist state emblem, a rising sun over an open book.
af.najibullah: Under Najibullah the state emblem was softened: the red star and the open Koran were removed as the government reached for Islamic legitimacy.
af.temp1992: In the chaos of 1992 a temporary flag appeared, green-white-black lettered with the Shahada and the takbir, "God is great".
af.temp1992b: A second temporary flag followed the same year, the writing dropped and the stripes reordered to black-white-green, the plain banner of the mujahideen government.
af.islamicstate: When the mujahideen took Kabul in 1992, the Islamic State of Afghanistan raised a green-white-black tricolour, its inscriptions gathered into a central emblem.
af.talibanwhite: When the Taliban first took Kabul in 1996, their flag was plain white, the colour of their movement, with no writing at all.
af.talibanshahada: In 1997 the Taliban added the Shahada, the declaration of faith, in black across the white field, the design that would return in 2021.
af.talibangreen: A variant of the Taliban flag lettered the same Shahada in green instead of black.
af.post2001: With the Taliban driven out, the interim government briefly flew a restored version of the 1990s mujahideen flag.
af.ref2002: A new black-red-green tricolour followed, its emblem harking back to the 1930–1973 kingdom.
af.republic: In 2004 the Islamic Republic settled on the black-red-green vertical tricolour with a white mosque emblem, the flag most of the world still pictures for Afghanistan.
af.coloured: A variant of the republican flag showed the state emblem in full colour rather than white.
af.deepred: In 2013 the red was deepened and the emblem enlarged, the final form of the republic's flag, still flown by its representatives abroad.
af.current: In 2021 the Taliban's white banner replaced the tricolour: the Shahada, the Islamic declaration of faith, lettered in black on white. The black-red-green flag is still flown by the former republic's representatives abroad.
af.pashto: Some Islamic Emirate flags add a line of Pashto script beneath the Shahada.

fact.changes: Afghanistan is often credited with the most flag changes of any country in the 20th century, by common counts around nineteen, roughly a new flag every five years.
fact.colours: The tricolour carried a standard reading: black for the country's dark, occupied past, red for the blood shed winning independence, and green for hope, prosperity and Islam.
fact.mosque: The recurring emblem is a mosque drawn straight on, showing the mihrab (the niche that points to Mecca) and the minbar (the pulpit), usually wrapped in wheat, a motif on Afghan flags since the early 1900s.
fact.longlived: The Kingdom's tricolour (1931–1973) was easily Afghanistan's longest-serving flag, lasting over forty years. Most of the others survived only a handful.
fact.white: The white Shahada flag has deep roots: white recalls the banners of the early Islamic caliphates. The Taliban first raised this design in the 1990s, and returned it in 2021.
fact.standards: Beyond the national flag, Afghanistan's rulers flew their own personal standards, distinct banners for the king or president, usually red or black and charged with the state arms.

country.sh: Saint Helena, Ascension and Tristan da Cunha
country.gs: South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands
